Lazy on-demand indexing. No bulk replication. First query for a block, transaction, or address fetches from the backend, persists viaUPSERT in the Database, and serves subsequent requests from cache until TTL expires.

Multi-backend failover. Three backends (Blockfrost, Koios, Ogmios). The orchestrator picks the right one per request type (Ogmios for live UTxOs, Blockfrost for historical) and fails over on timeout. Transparent to the consumer.

Database-agnostic. CDS entities and standard CAP persistence. SQLite locally, HANA on BTP. No code change, no schema migration. CDS compiler handles the DDL.

TRACE: a concrete use case

To make this less abstract and to test the plugin firsthand, I built a second CAP app: TRACE, a Fiori frontend for pharmaceutical supply chain tracking. TRACE just consumes @odatano/core as a plugin and can use all of the available services for interacting with the Cardano blockchain without having to write one line of custom blockchain code.

Business Scenario: A manufacturer produces a batch. Regulation requires tamper-proof evidence of every custody transfer from the manufacturer to the distributor to the pharmacy. Nobody can rewrite history afterward.

The manufacturer mints the batch as an NFT via ODATANOs BuildMintTransaction OData Action. The NFT carries an inline datum (manufacturer ID, batch ID, custody state) written on-chain at creation.

Each Batch handover calls the OData Action BuildPlutusSpendTransaction. The Plutus V3 Cardano Smartcontract validator enforces on-chain who's allowed to transfer. inlineDatumJson carries the updated custody state to the continuing output.

Any party, manufacturer, regulator, pharmacy, or end consumer can read the full custody chain without access to anyone's SAP system. Because the data lives on Cardano, not in any vendor database.

Conclusion

ODATANO started as a personal question: could a public Blockchain like Cardano, with smart contracts & transaction logic, feel like another OData Service? After half a year of building, I think the answer is yes. Three OData services, familiar CAP patterns, and TRACE as proof that a real consumer app doesn't need a single line of blockchain-specific code.  What excites me most is that this way, Cardano just shows up as another service in the stack, with the same $metadata, the same query syntax, the same handlers as any other CAP service, just with tamper-proof storage and cryptographic guarantees by design.

Status

Funded by Cardano Catalyst Fund 14 (Open Developers). Apache 2.0. Final milestone approved late April, no reviewer refusals, about two months before the deadline.

  • Version: v1.7.8 (@odatano/core on npm)
  • Tests: 1,285 tests across 31 suites, 97% statement coverage
  • Cardano Networks: mainnet, preview, preprod
  • Backends: Blockfrost, Koios, Ogmios (with a real Cardano node)
  • CAP compatibility: @SAP/cds 9.x, Node.js 18+
  • Main Repo: github.com/ODATANO/ODATANO
  • TRACE (Example Consumer App):  TRACE 
  • Homepage: https://odatano.dev/ 

If anyone wants to poke at it, try it, tear it apart, please do. I'm especially interested in feedback from people deep in CAP, because I'm never quite sure which patterns are the most idiomatic. Issues, threads, and messages are all welcome.

Thanks and best regards

Max

entry.title: Disabling CSRF Token Validation in SAP for Testing with Postman and External Applications
entry.content:
Introduction

When working with SAP OData services, developers frequently encounter CSRF token validation errors while testing APIs using tools like Postman or integrating with third-party applications. These errors commonly occur during POST, PUT, PATCH, or DELETE operations because SAP Gateway enforces CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection for security purposes.

In this blog, we will walk through:

  • What CSRF token validation is in SAP
  • Why Postman and external applications face CSRF-related issues
  • How to disable CSRF token validation for a specific SAP OData service
  • Testing the service using SAP Gateway Client and Postman

What is CSRF Token Validation in SAP?

CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection is a security mechanism implemented by SAP Gateway to prevent unauthorized requests from malicious applications.

For modifying operations such as:

  • POST
  • PUT
  • PATCH
  • DELETE

SAP requires a valid X-CSRF-Token header.

If the token is missing or invalid, SAP returns errors such as:

  • 403 Forbidden
  • CSRF token validation failed

This issue is very common while testing APIs from:

  • Postman
  • External middleware applications
  • Custom UI applications
  • Third-party integrations

Common Issue Faced in Postman

Most developers directly trigger a POST or PUT request from Postman without first fetching the CSRF token.

As a result, SAP Gateway rejects the request due to missing token validation.

Go to transaction code: SICF

SICF ->service->GUI Configuration

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Setting the value to 0 disables CSRF token validation for the service

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Test the Service in SAP Gateway Client:

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Testing from Postman

After disabling CSRF validation, you can directly perform:

  • POST requests
  • PUT requests
  • PATCH requests
  • DELETE requests

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

Efficient API testing is essential for successful SAP integrations, and managing CSRF token validation is a key part of that process. Whether you are working with Postman, middleware platforms, or custom applications, knowing how to configure and troubleshoot CSRF settings can save considerable development time. Always ensure that any security relaxation performed for testing is carefully controlled and documented to avoid risks in productive environments.

ABAP Development 

OData 

SAP NetWeaver Application Server for ABAP 

NW ABAP Gateway (OData)

entry.title: Querying SAP SuccessFactors in Natural Language with Claude Desktop and the SF-MCP Server
entry.content:
How to connect an open-source Model Context Protocol server to Claude Desktop and interact with your SuccessFactors tenant using plain English - no OData knowledge required.

What is SF-MCP?

SF-MCP is an open-source Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that bridges Claude — Anthropic's AI assistant — to SAP SuccessFactors via its OData APIs. Instead of hand-crafting $filter expressions or memorising entity names, you just ask Claude in plain English and it translates your question into the right API call automatically.

 

Example interaction

You: "Who on the Engineering team has a work anniversary this month?"

Claude: Found 3 upcoming anniversaries — Jane Smith (5 years, March 12), 
Bob Johnson (2 years, March 18), Alice Chen (10 years, March 25).

The project lives at github.com/aiadiguru2025/sf-mcp and ships 43 purpose-built HR tools across 13 categories — covering everything from employee lookup and RBP security audits to hiring pipelines, time-off balances, and Integration Center job monitoring.

MCP is an open standard that lets AI assistants connect to external data sources in a secure, standardised way. Claude Desktop is the desktop client that makes it easy to wire up MCP servers locally without any coding.

Prerequisites

Before starting, make sure you have the following:

✅ Claude Desktop installed (download from claude.ai/download)
✅ Python 3.10 or newer — check with python3 --version
✅ Git installed
✅ A SAP SuccessFactors account with API access enabled
✅ Your company ID, data center code (e.g. DC55 for Frankfurt), and API user credentials

Note: Credentials are passed per-request and never stored in the config file. SF-MCP uses per-request authentication, so you will supply your user ID and password when you ask Claude each question.

Step-by-step setup

 

1. Install uv and clone the repository

SF-MCP uses uv, a fast Python package manager. Install it first, then clone the repo.

# Install uv (macOS / Linux)
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Install uv (Windows PowerShell)
powershell -c "irm https://astral.sh/uv/install.ps1 | iex"

# Clone the repo
git clone https://github.com/aiadiguru2025/sf-mcp.git
cd sf-mcp

# Install Python dependencies
uv sync

 
2. Find the path to the uv executable

Claude Desktop needs the absolute path to uv to launch the server. Run:

# macOS / Linux
which uv
# → /Users/yourname/.local/bin/uv

# Windows PowerShell
Get-Command uv | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Source
# → C:\Users\yourname\.local\bin\uv.exe

Copy this path — you will need it in the next step.

 
 
3. Edit the Claude Desktop configuration file

Open the config file for your operating system in any text editor:

# macOS
~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json

# Windows
%APPDATA%\Claude\claude_desktop_config.json

Add the sf-mcp entry inside mcpServers. Replace the paths with the values you found in step 2:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "sf-mcp": {
      "command": "/Users/yourname/.local/bin/uv",
      "args": [
        "--directory",
        "/Users/yourname/projects/sf-mcp",
        "run",
        "main.py"
      ]
    }
  }
}
Tip (Windows): Use forward slashes or double backslashes in the JSON paths, e.g. C:\\Users\\yourname\\projects\\sf-mcp
 
4. Restart Claude Desktop and verify the connection

Fully quit Claude Desktop (not just close the window), then reopen it. After a few seconds you should see a hammer icon (🔨) appear in the message input area. Click it — you should see all 43 SF-MCP tools listed.

 
 
5. Run your first query

Now ask Claude a question in plain English. You will need to provide your SuccessFactors connection details the first time — Claude will prompt you for them or you can include them upfront:

 
 

The 43 tools at a glance

SF-MCP groups its tools into 13 categories. Here are some of the most useful ones for day-to-day HR and admin work:

Natural language query examples

Security considerations

Key security features you should be aware of:

🔐 Per-request credentials — your username and password are never stored in the config file or cached to disk. They are passed with each API call and masked in audit logs.
🛡️  Input validation — 10 regex-based validators protect against OData injection. Filter expressions, entity names, and $select/$orderby values are all sanitised.
🧩  XXE-safe XML parsing — the server uses defusedxml to prevent XML external entity attacks when processing OData metadata responses.
📋 Structured audit log — every tool call is logged with a request ID, timestamp, duration, and parameter summary (passwords redacted).

Troubleshooting tips

HTTP 401 — Authentication Error

Verify you are passing the user ID without the @instance suffix. For example use api_admin, not api_admin@ACME_CORP.

Server disconnects immediately in Claude Desktop

Server Disconnects in Claude Desktop

Run which uv again and confirm the path in your config is exactly right. Then test the server manually: uv run mcp dev main.py — this opens the MCP Inspector in your browser so you can call tools interactively.

Rate limit / 429 errors

Rate Limit / 429 Errors

The server automatically retries up to 3 times with a 5-second back-off. If you hit limits frequently, set SF_CACHE_TTL_DEFAULT in a .env file to cache read-heavy responses like picklists and metadata.

Final thoughts

I have created SF-MCP as one of the most complete open-source SuccessFactors API integrations available. Forty-three well-designed tools, solid input validation, production-grade caching and rate limiting, and a clean architecture that is easy to extend, all built on FastMCP, the leading Python MCP framework.

If you are an SF admin, HR tech consultant, or developer who regularly digs through OData APIs, this is worth setting up. The ability to ask Claude "compare User entity config between dev and prod" and get a structured diff instantly is a genuine time-saver.

Give it a ⭐ on GitHub and feel free to contribute - the project is MIT-licensed and I have laid out clear contribution guidelines in the README: github.com/aiadiguru2025/sf-mcp

Disclaimer: 

This project is provided for educational and experimental purposes only. It is offered as-is, with no warranties — express or implied. The author assumes no liability for any damages or issues arising from its use, including data loss, unintended API activity, or security vulnerabilities. Always validate behaviour in a non-production environment before wider use.

Before connecting SF-MCP repo to any SuccessFactors environment, please keep the following in mind:

  • Always start with a sandbox or preview instance - never point this at production as your first test
  • Grant your API user the minimum permissions needed and nothing more
  • Treat all tool outputs as informational — verify before taking any action based on them
  • Every query makes real, live OData API calls to your tenant. There is no simulation or dry-run mode

The author accepts no liability for any damages - direct or indirect - resulting from the use of this software. SF-MCP is an independent community project and is not affiliated with, endorsed by, or supported by SAP SE or Anthropic.

entry.title: Dynamic Criticality in SAP CAP Using Fiori Elements and Node.js
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a CAP application recently, I came across a requirement where records needed to be highlighted based on their status. The business users wanted a quick way to identify whether an order was completed, still in progress, or blocked without having to read through each record individually.

My first thought was to store a criticality value directly in the database, but that didn't feel like the right approach because the value was purely UI-related and depended on the business status. After exploring a few options, I found that CAP and Fiori Elements provide a clean way to calculate criticality dynamically in the backend and display semantic colors automatically in the UI.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to achieve this using SAP CAP, Node.js, OData annotations, and Fiori Elements. By the end, you'll have a working example where criticality is calculated at runtime and reflected directly in your Fiori application.

Why Use Dynamic Criticality?

In many business applications, the visual status of a record depends on changing business conditions. For example, an order may move from "In Process" to "Completed", or an expense request may be "Approved" or "Rejected".

Storing criticality values in the database means the values must be maintained whenever the business status changes. By calculating criticality dynamically in the CAP service, the UI always reflects the latest business state while keeping the database model focused on business data.

Step 1: Create a CAP Project

mkdir criticality-demo 
cd criticality-demo 
cds init 
npm install 
npm install sqlite3

Step 2: Create the Data Model

Create the file db/schema.cds

namespace demo.criticality;

entity SalesOrders {
  key ID           : Integer;
      CustomerName : String(100);
      Amount       : Decimal(10,2);
      Currency     : String(3);
      Status       : String(30);
}

Step 3: Add Sample Data

          Create the file:  db/data/demo.criticality-SalesOrders.csv

ID;CustomerName;Amount;Currency;Status
1;ABC Corporation;1200.00;INR;Completed
2;Global Traders;5000.00;INR;In Process
3;Tech Solutions;800.00;INR;Blocked
4;Amazon;900.00;INR;In Process

Step 4: Create an OData Service

          Create the file srv/service.cds.

using demo.criticality as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity SalesOrders as projection on db.SalesOrders;
}

Step 5: Configure Fiori Annotations

          Create the file app/annotations.cds.

using CatalogService as service from '../srv/service';

annotate service.SalesOrders with @(
  UI.LineItem : [
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Customer',
      Value : CustomerName
    },
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Amount',
      Value : Amount
    },
    {
      $Type : 'UI.DataField',
      Label : 'Status',
      Value : Status,
      Criticality : Criticality,
      CriticalityRepresentation : #WithIcon
    }
  ],

  UI.SelectionFields : [
    CustomerName,
    Status
  ]
);

          The most important configuration is:

Criticality : Criticality

This tells SAP Fiori to use the Criticality value exposed by the OData service to determine the color and icon displayed for the Status field.

Step 6: Create a Fiori Elements Application

Open the SAP Fiori Application Generator and select:

  • Template: List Report Object Page
  • Data Source: Use a Local CAP Project
  • Service: CatalogService
  • Entity: SalesOrders

Complete the wizard to generate the Fiori Elements application.

Step 7: Run the Application

Start the CAP application:

cds watch

Step 8: Make Criticality Dynamic Using Node.js

Since Criticality is calculated dynamically, we do not need to persist it in the database. Instead, we expose it as a computed field in the service projection and populate it at runtime based on the business status.

A useful side effect of this approach is that if the business decides to change the criticality rules in the future, only the CAP service logic needs to be updated, without requiring database changes or frontend modifications.

At this stage, we will update the service definition created in Step 4 to expose a computed Criticality field.

using demo.criticality as db from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
  entity SalesOrders as projection on db.SalesOrders {
    *,
    null as Criticality : Integer
  };
}

           Then Create srv/service.js:

module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function () {
  this.after('READ', 'SalesOrders', (orders) => {
    const list = Array.isArray(orders) ? orders : [orders];

    for (const order of list) {
      switch (order.Status) {
        case 'Completed':
          order.Criticality = 3;
          break;
        case 'In Process':
          order.Criticality = 2;
          break;
        case 'Blocked':
          order.Criticality = 1;
          break;
        default:
          order.Criticality = 0;
      }
    }
  });
});

With this implementation, criticality is calculated at runtime based on the business status rather than being stored in the database.

As shown below, Fiori automatically renders different semantic colors based on the Criticality value calculated in the CAP service. Completed orders appear in green, In Process orders appear in yellow, and Blocked orders appear in red.

Understanding Criticality Values

SAP Fiori uses the following standard criticality values:

Criticality Value Meaning Color Typical Usage

0NeutralGrayDefault or informational status
1NegativeRedError, Rejected, Blocked
2CriticalOrange/YellowWarning, Pending, In Process
3PositiveGreenSuccess, Approved, Completed

Conclusion

What I liked about this approach is that the database remains focused on business data while the CAP service handles the UI-specific logic. Instead of maintaining criticality values in a table, we can derive them dynamically from the status and expose them through the service whenever the data is requested.

One thing that took me some time to figure out was that the calculated field must also be exposed in the service projection. Without that step, the application receives the status value but not the criticality information needed for the semantic colors.

Although this example uses sales order statuses, the same pattern can be applied to approval workflows, expense reports, support tickets, or any scenario where users need a quick visual indication of the current state of a record. I hope this helps anyone looking to implement dynamic criticality in their own CAP applications.

entry.title: Qualifications Replication as Skills from SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud to SAP Field Service Management
entry.content:
• Context
  • Scope
  • Connecting SAP to FSM
  • Single Company Setup
  • Multiple Company Setup
  • Scale replication
  • Step 1: Create Scales in S/4HANA
  • Step 2: Replicate Scales to FSM
  • Deleting Scales in FSM
  • Error Log and Troubleshooting — Scales
  • Qualification / Skill Replication
  • Step 1: Create a Qualification Group
  • Step 2: Create Individual Qualifications (Skills)
  • Manual Replication (On Demand)
  • Deleting a Qualification Group or Qualification in FSM
  • Error Log and Troubleshooting — Qualifications
  • Skill Assignment to Employees
  • Step 1: Create the Employee Master Record
  • Step 2: Assign Skills (Qualifications) to the Employee
  • Step 3: Replicate the Business Partner (Employee) to FSM
  • Skill Assignment to Service Orders
  • Skills assignment at header level
  • Skills assignment at item level
  • Skills as shown in FSM activity:
  • References & Useful Links

Context

Imagine a customer calls in because a piece of hydraulic equipment just broke down on their factory floor. Your dispatcher needs to send someone out — fast. But not just anyone. They need a technician who actually knows hydraulics. Send the wrong person and the job doesn't get fixed on the first visit, the customer is frustrated, and your team has wasted a trip.

This is the problem that skill matching solves. Your company already knows which technicians hold which qualifications — that information lives in your SAP system. Your field scheduling tool (SAP Field Service Management, or FSM for short) is where dispatchers actually assign jobs. The goal of everything in this guide is simply to connect those two: get the skill information from SAP into FSM so the scheduler can automatically find the right person for every job.

Scope

Scope of this document 

  1. Scale and Proficiency replication
  2. Qualification and Qualification Group replication
  3. Assignment of Qualification/Skill to Employee
  4. Assignment to Qualification/Skill to Service Order and Activity 

NOTE - Qualification assignment to Maintenance Orders are not yet supported 

Connecting SAP to FSM

  • Refer setup-for-s-4hana-and-sap-field-service-management for management for how to maintain FSM system details in S/4 HANA systemHAN. ..
  • Configure the FSM replication settings in the SAP IMG by navigating to: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Integration. This activity sets the RFC destination, Account/Company IDs, Client Version, and the REST API paths used to communicate with the FSM cloud tenant.

     

Fig 1: SPRO Path to Configure FSM Integration

 

Fig 2: FSM Config parameters

 

  •  To monitor the data transfer to FSM, you must extract the configuration settings delivered by SAP for the  SAP Application Interface Framework  (SAP AIF). SAP recommends extracting the delivered SAP AIF configuration settings in your development system and transporting them to downstream quality and production systems. After extracting the SAP AIF content, you can monitor the data transfer to data ingestion for industry cloud solutions by using message logs generated during replication. Refer Monitoring Using AIF in SAP S/4HANA 

Single Company Setup

  • For a single-company deployment, provide the FSM Account ID and Company ID in the same IMG node: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Integration. These values uniquely identify your FSM tenant and ensure that replicated objects are routed to the correct company context.

Fig 3: Single Company settings

 

 

Multiple Company Setup

  • When multiple SAP company codes need to replicate data to different FSM companies, define the company-to-company mapping in: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Configure Multiple Company Replication. Each entry maps an S/4HANA company code to its corresponding FSM company, allowing fine-grained control over which data is shared with which FSM tenant. 

Fig 4: SPRO path to define multi-company settings 

Scale replication

  • Scale replication is a ‘manual’ activity — unlike Qualification replication, it does not trigger automatically when a Scale is created or changed. You must explicitly execute the replication program described below.
  • Before proceeding, verify that all prerequisite configuration steps have been completed. Refer to the ‘Configure Replication of Scales’ IMG activity for the full checklist (RFC destination, API paths, Account/Company IDs). Configure Replication of Scales

Step 1: Create Scales in S/4HANA

  • Open the IMG activity ‘Maintain Scales’ by navigating to: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Basic Functions → Qualifications → Maintain Scales. This is the starting point for defining all proficiency scales that will later be replicated to FSM as ‘Levels’ within a skill category.

Fig 5: SPRO path to define Scale

  •   Press ‘New Entries’ and provide a unique Scale ID (e.g., 51) and a descriptive Scale Name (e.g., Hydraulic_Technician). Save the entry. The system will then prompt you to define the scale type — choose ‘Quality Scale’ (as opposed to Quantity Scale) because FSM skills use named proficiency levels rather than numeric ranges.

 

Fig 6: Define Scale

  •   Select the ‘Characteristic Values’ node in the dialog structure on the left. Add one row per proficiency level, assigning a sequential ID and a meaningful name. For example, for the Hydraulic_Technician scale: ID 1 = Basic, ID 2 = Advanced, ID 3 = Experienced. These names will appear verbatim as skill levels inside FSM.

 

Fig 7: Maintain Scale Proficiencies

  •  Optionally, select the ‘Proficiency descriptions’ node to add longer descriptions for each level (e.g., “Awareness; requires supervision” for the Basic level). These descriptions provide additional context to dispatchers when matching technician skills to service order requirements.
  • Create a second Scale to cover a different skill category. For example, create ‘Safety_Certifications’ (ID 52) as a Quality Scale with two characteristic values: ID 1 = Pass, ID 2 = Fail. This binary scale is appropriate for certification checks such as safety training or compliance tests.

Fig 9: Another Scale

  

 

Step 2: Replicate Scales to FSM

  • Once the scales are created and saved, replicate them to FSM by executing the IMG activity ‘Replicate Scales’ found at: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Integration with Other SAP Solutions → SAP Field Service Management → Master Data Replication → Replicate Scales. This launches a selection screen where you specify which scales to send.

Fig 10: SPRO path to Replicate Scales

 

  •  Enter one or more Scale IDs in the ‘Scales ID (Multiple)’ field or use the F4 value help to select from the list. Ensure the ‘Test Mode’ checkbox is —unchecked— so the replication is executed for real. Click the Execute button (F8) to start the process.

Fig 11: Scale replication parameters

  •  The system processes the replication synchronously and immediately displays an execution log. Green information messages confirm success; red error messages indicate failures that need to be investigated. Key log messages to look for include: “Scale Found”, “Relevant scale data found!”, “RFC destination is maintained”, “Account and company determination is successful”, and “Scale Replications started”.

 

Fig 12: Scale Execution log

  •  After successful replication, open the FSM application and navigate to Settings and Configuration → Master Data → Scales. Both replicated scales (e.g., Hydraulic_Technician and Safety_Certifications) should now be visible in the list, along with their proficiency levels on the right-hand panel.

 

Fig 13.1: Scales in FSM

 

  Fig 13.2: Another Scale in FSM

 

Deleting Scales in FSM

  • To remove all scales from FSM, first delete or deactivate the relevant Scale entries in S/4HANA, then re-run the same ‘Replicate Scales’ IMG activity. This time, leave the Scale ID field empty and check the ‘Delete Scale/Proficiency’ checkbox before executing. This instructs the program to clear all existing scale data in FSM.
  • After the deletion run is completed, you can re-run the replication (without the checkbox and with specific Scale IDs) to reload only the desired scales into FSM. This is the recommended approach for refreshing scale data after bulk changes.

Error Log and Troubleshooting — Scales

  • Scale replication communicates with FSM asynchronously via HTTP. If an HTTP-level error occurs (e.g., authentication failure, network timeout, or malformed payload), it will not appear in the ABAP short dump but can be traced in the AIF (Application Interface Framework) error log. Navigate to transaction ‘AIF’ or use the Monitoring and Error Handling screen, filtering on Namespace ‘/FSM’ and Interface Name ‘SCALE_OUT’.

Fig 14: AIF log messages

  •  In the AIF error log, select a failed message from the left-hand panel. The right-hand panel displays the full JSON payload that was sent to FSM, along with detailed error information. Common issues include invalid Scale IDs, missing RFC destination configuration, incorrect Account/Company mapping, or FSM API rate limiting. After fixing the root cause, you can restart failed messages directly from the AIF screen

Fig 15: AIF log in detail

 

Qualification / Skill Replication

  • Qualification (also referred to as ‘Skill’ in FSM) replication is triggered both automatically (real-time, whenever a Qualification Group or Qualification is created or changed in S/4HANA) and manually (on demand via transaction DRFOUT). This dual-mode approach ensures the FSM skill catalog stays in sync with the S/4HANA qualification catalog without requiring scheduled batch jobs.
  • Before creating qualifications, verify that all prerequisite configuration steps have been completed. Refer to the IMG activity ‘Configure Replication of Qualifications’ for the full checklist, including the Outbound Implementation settings and DRF (Data Replication Framework) configuration: Configure Replication of Qualifications

Step 1: Create a Qualification Group

  • A Qualification Group acts as a category or container for related individual qualifications. Create a new group using the IMG activity: SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → Service → Basic Functions → Qualifications → Edit Qualifications Catalog, or by calling transaction ‘OOQA’ directly. The Qualifications Catalog is structured as a tree; groups are the parent nodes.

Fig 16.1: SPRO path to define Qualification group

  • In the Qualifications Catalog screen, click the ‘Create’ button and choose ‘Qualification group’ from the popup. Enter a short ID (e.g., QK prefix is auto-assigned), a descriptive name (e.g., ‘Hydraulic Systems’), a validity period, and optionally a longer description. In the ‘Scale’ dropdown, select the scale you created earlier (e.g., 51 Hydraulic_Technician). This links the proficiency levels to all qualifications within this group.

 

Fig 16.2: Maintain Qualification group attributes

  •  After saving the Qualification Group, switch to the ‘Proficiencies’ tab in the dialog. The proficiency levels are automatically inherited from the selected scale (e.g., 1 = Basic, 2 = Advanced, 3 = Experienced). Select the radio button next to the level that represents the ‘default’ required proficiency — this default is used when a service order does not specify an explicit proficiency requirement.

 

Fig 16.3: Maintain proficiencies

Step 2: Create Individual Qualifications (Skills)

  • Individual qualifications represent specific skills or competencies (e.g., ‘Component maintenance’, ‘Hydraulic System design’). To create one, place the cursor on the parent Qualification Group in the catalog tree and press ‘Create’. In the popup, choose ‘Qualification’ (not ‘Qualification group’). Fill in a name, description, and confirm the inherited scale.

Fig 17: Define Qualification/Skill

  •  Repeat this process to build a complete skill catalog. For example, under the ‘Hydraulic Systems’ group you might add: Hose & Fitting Assembly, Hydraulic System design, Pressure testing & Fault finding, and Component maintenance. Create additional groups as needed (e.g., ‘Safety Certifications’ linked to scale 52) with their own qualifications (e.g., Forklift/MEWP license, Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)). The resulting tree structure mirrors the skill hierarchy that will be visible in FSM.

Fig 18: Qualifications defined under Qualification group

  •  Qualification replication to FSM is triggered automatically upon save. Once replication completes successfully, open the FSM application and navigate to Master Data → Skills. You will see the qualification groups mapped as ‘skill categories’ and the individual qualifications mapped as ‘skills’, each with their assigned scale levels.

Fig 19.1: Skills in FSM

 

 

Fig 19.2: Skills in FSM

 

 

Manual Replication (On Demand)

  • While automatic replication handles most day-to-day synchronization, you may need to trigger replication manually — for example after a bulk import, a system migration or to force-resend a specific qualification. Use transaction ‘DRFOUT’ (Execute Data Replication) for this purpose.
  • Open transaction DRFOUT. Set the ‘Replication Model’ to ‘FSM_INT’ (RM for BO replication to FSM) and the ‘Outbound Implementation’ to ‘ORG_REPL’. Set the ‘Replication Mode’ to ‘Manual’ and tick the ‘Manual Replication Filter Criteria Available’ checkbox. Then click the ‘Manual Replication Filter Criteria’ button at the top of the screen.

Fig 20: DRFOUT parameters for Qualification replication

  •  In the filter selection screen, expand the ‘Filter for org data’ node. Enter the Qualification Group ID or Qualification ID in the ‘Object ID’ field (use F4 help to browse). Set the ‘Object type’ accordingly (e.g., ‘OK’ for Qualification Group, ‘Q’ for individual Qualification). Save the filter, return to the previous screen, and click ‘Execute’ (F8).

Fig 21: Enter IDs to replicate

  •  The system displays a replication log screen. Green messages indicate that the qualification data was successfully packaged and sent to FSM. If errors appear, check the AIF error log (see ‘Error Log and Troubleshooting’ section below).

Fig 22: DRF Execution status

 

Deleting a Qualification Group or Qualification in FSM

  • Deletions and changes are handled in real time. When you delete or change a Qualification Group or individual Qualification in S/4HANA (via OOQA or the IMG activity), the change event is immediately captured by the Data Replication Framework and sent to FSM. There is no need to manually trigger a replication after a deletion. If an error occurs during the sync, investigate it in the AIF error log described below.

Error Log and Troubleshooting — Qualifications

  • Qualification replication communicates with FSM asynchronously via HTTP. If an error occurs at the HTTP transport layer, it will be recorded in the AIF error log. Navigate to AIF Monitoring and Error Handling and filter on Namespace ‘/FSM’ and Interface Name ‘QUAL_OUT’ to see qualification-specific messages. Select the date range that covers when the replication was triggered.

Fig 24: Error log in AIF

  • Expand a failed message in the left panel to see its individual transfer records. Select a record to view the JSON payload and the specific error in the right panel. The payload follows the ‘PAYLOAD:/AIF/JSON_LINE_S’ structure, showing the qualification tree sent to FSM. After diagnosing the issue (e.g., missing skill category mapping, FSM authentication error, or invalid external ID), fix the root cause and restart the failed message from the AIF screen.

Fig 24: AIF log details

 

 

Skill Assignment to Employees

Step 1: Create the Employee Master Record

  • Employee records in S/4HANA are created and maintained in HR Master Data transaction ‘PA30’ (Maintain HR Master Data). Open PA30, leave the Personnel Number field blank, select the ‘Actions’ infotype from the list, and press ‘Create’ to start a new hiring action.

Fig 25: PA30

  •  In the ‘Create Actions’ screen, set the Action Type (e.g., 01 Hiring), Reason for Action, and the Start Date. Proceed through the infotype screens to provide: Personal Data (name, birthdate, gender, language, nationality), Organizational Assignment (company code, personnel area, employee group/subgroup, position, org unit), and Communication details (email, SAP user ID link if applicable). Save each infotype screen to create the complete employee record.

Fig 26: Employee attributes

 

 

Fig 26: Employee details

  •  If the employee will log on to the SAP system, link the employee record to their SAP user ID using the ‘Communication’ infotype (IT0105, subtype 0001 SY-BNAME). This connection is required for certain self-service and approval workflows.

Fig 27: Link SAP User ID to employee

  •  After the employee record is saved, run program ‘/SHCM/RH_SYNC_BUPA_EMPL_SINGLE’ with the new Personnel Number. This program creates or updates the Business Partner (BP) record in S/4HANA that corresponds to this employee. The BP record is the object that is ultimately replicated to FSM as a ‘Person’ (technician).

Fig 28: Create BP using employee

  • You can verify that the BP was created successfully by checking the CDS view ‘I_BUSINESSUSER’. Search for the employee’s last name or Personnel Number. The result should show one row with the BP GUID, Business Partner number, and the linked SAP username, confirming the employee-BP linkage is intact.

Fig 29: BP and Employee ID in CDS view

 

 Step 2: Assign Skills (Qualifications) to the Employee

  • Use transaction ‘PPPM’ (Maintain Qualifications Profile) to assign qualifications to the employee. Open PPPM and switch to the ‘Person’ search tab. Search by name or Personnel Number. Select the employee from the ‘Hit List’ at the bottom-left and open the record in Change mode. Navigate to the ‘Qualifications’ tab.

Fig 30: PPPM Transaction to define Qualifications

  •  Click ‘Create’ to add a new qualification entry. In the dialog, search for the qualification either by ‘Description’ (e.g., ‘Forklift / MEWP license’) or by ‘Qualification group’ (e.g., ‘Safety Certifications’). Once a qualification is selected, the ‘Proficiency’ dropdown becomes active — choose the appropriate level (e.g., Pass). Set the validity dates and save. Repeat for each skill the employee holds. The assigned qualifications will be automatically replicated to FSM as part of the person’s skill profile.

Fig 31: Qualifications assigned to Employee

  Step 3: Replicate the Business Partner (Employee) to FSM

  • While qualification changes trigger automatic replication, the initial BP replication must be triggered manually. Open transaction ‘DRFOUT’ and set: Replication Model = FSM_INT, Outbound Implementation = 986_3 (or the BP-specific implementation in your system). Set Replication Mode to ‘Manual’ and click ‘Manual Replication Filter Criteria’.

Fig 32: DRFOUT for BP

  •  In the filter screen, enter the Business Partner number in the ‘Business Partner’ field (obtained from the /SHCM/RH_SYNC_BUPA_EMPL_SINGLE run or from I_BUSINESSUSER). Save the filter, return to the main screen, and execute. 

Fig 34.1: DRF execution status

  Fig 34.2 DRF execution status details screen

 Fig 34.3: Outbound Proxy Message status

  • After successful replication, open FSM and navigate to Master Data → People. The employee should appear as an active person entry with their skills visible under the ‘Skills’ tab of their People records.

Fig 35.1: Employee in FSM

 

Fig 35.2: Skills assigned to BP in FSM

Skill Assignment to Service Orders

  • Skill requirements can be attached to a Service Order to indicate which technician competencies are needed for the job. Open the relevant Service Order in the S/4HANA Service application, navigate to the ‘Qualification Requirements’ tab on the Service Order Header, and click ‘Edit’. Assign newly created skills to Service Order Header and Item in ‘Qualification Requirements’ tab, save and ‘Release’  the Service Order. FSM will use these skill requirements during scheduling to match the order with qualified technicians.

 Skills assignment at header level

Fig 36.1: Skills in Service Order header

 

Skills assignment at item level

Fig 36.2: Skills at Service Order item

 

Skills as shown in FSM activity:

Fig 36.3: Skills in FSM Activity

 

References & Useful Links

  • SAP Help Portal –    Field Service Management Integration

entry.title: Building an Inventory System with RAP on BTP Trial — CDS Views Explained
entry.content:
Introduction

When people talk about RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming Model), the conversation usually jumps straight to behavior definitions, actions, and determinations. The CDS layer gets a brief mention and everyone moves on.

That's a mistake.

The CDS view architecture is the contract your entire application is built on. Get it right and everything above it — behaviors, OData services, Fiori UIs — falls into place naturally. Get it wrong and you'll spend hours debugging problems that were actually design issues.

In this post I'll walk through the complete CDS layer of a Mini Inventory & Stock Movement System I built on SAP BTP Trial, covering four entities across three business objects. I'll explain the design decisions, not just the code.

The System at a Glance

The application manages warehouse stock movements. Three business objects cover the full domain:

Business Object Interface View Database Table
Material MasterZI_MiniMM_MaterialZRMZ_MM_MATERIAL
Storage LocationZI_MiniMM_StorageZRMZ_MM_STORAGE
Stock Document (Header)ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentZRMZ_MM_DOC_HDR
Stock Document (Item)ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItemZRMZ_MM_DOC_ITEM
Current Stock (read-only)ZI_MiniMM_CurrentStockZRMZ_MM_STOCK

Each entity has a matching projection view (ZC_ prefix) used to expose the OData service.

The Two-Layer Architecture

RAP enforces a clean separation between two CDS view types. Understanding why this separation exists is more important than understanding the syntax.

Interface views (ZI_) are the authoritative source of truth. They sit directly on the database tables and carry everything the framework needs to understand your data: semantic annotations, associations, compositions, and quantity/currency references. These views should be stable — changing them has downstream impact.

Projection views (ZC_) consume the interface views and add presentation concerns: the provider contract, @Metadata.allowExtensions for metadata extensions, and the redirection of compositions so that OData navigation points to the correct projection layer. Projection views can evolve more freely.

A useful mental model: ZI_ is the API contract, ZC_ is the UI adapter.

Composition: Stock Document Header and Item

The most important structural relationship in this model is the header-item composition between ZI_MiniMM_StockDocument and ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem.

In the header (root entity):

 
 
abap
composition [0..*] of ZI_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem    as _Items

In the item (child entity):

 
 
abap
association to parent ZI_MiniMM_StockDocument  as _Header  on $projection.DocumentID = _Header.DocumentID

The [0..*] cardinality means a document can have zero or more items — appropriate here since a newly created document starts empty. The parent association on the item side creates the back-navigation link.

Why does this matter? Composition in RAP is not just a modelling convenience. It has runtime implications: the managed runtime treats the composition tree as a single transactional unit. When you delete a header, items are automatically deleted. When you lock a header for editing, the entire tree is locked. This is the correct behaviour for a stock document.

Associations: Two Aliases, One Target View

The item view introduces an interesting challenge: a stock movement can have both a source storage location and a target storage location, and both must be validated against the same ZI_MiniMM_Storage entity.

The solution is two separate associations pointing to the same underlying view:

 
 
abap
association [1..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Material as _Material  on $projection.MaterialID = _Material.MaterialID
association [0..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Storage as _SourceStorage  on $projection.SourceStorage = _SourceStorage.StorageID
association [0..1] to ZI_MiniMM_Storage as _TargetStorage  on $projection.TargetStorage = _TargetStorage.StorageID

A few design points worth noting here.

The _Material association is [1..1] — every item line must reference a valid material. _SourceStorage and _TargetStorage are both [0..1] because not every movement type uses both. A goods receipt (GR) has no source; a goods issue (GI) has no target. Making both optional at the CDS level correctly reflects the business reality.

The alias names (_SourceStorage, _TargetStorage) are different even though both navigate to ZI_MiniMM_Storage. This is required by the framework — CDS does not allow two associations with identical names on the same view, even if the target differs.

Semantic Annotations: Free Administrative Data

RAP's managed runtime will auto-populate administrative fields if you annotate them correctly. No custom code required.

 
 
abap
@Semantics.user.createdBy: truecreated_by      as CreatedBy,

@Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: truecreated_at      as CreatedAt,

@Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: truelast_changed_by as ChangedBy,

@Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: truelast_changed_at as ChangedAt

These annotations go on the interface view, not the projection. The framework reads them at runtime and fills the mapped database columns on every create and update operation.

The only requirement is that the underlying database table columns have compatible data types (SYUNAME, TIMESTAMPL respectively) and that you declare with_draft or standard managed behaviour in the behavior definition. If those conditions are met, this is genuinely zero-effort audit trail data.

Projection Views: What Most Tutorials Skip

Every interface view has a corresponding projection view. The projection view adds three things that the interface view cannot provide: the provider contract transactional_query declaration (required for OData V4 exposure), @Metadata.allowExtensions: true (required for metadata extensions to attach), and — crucially — the composition redirect.

The composition redirect is the step that most beginner tutorials either skip or explain poorly.

In the projection header:

 
 
abap
_Items : redirected to composition child ZC_MiniMM_StockDocumentItem

In the projection item:

 
 
abap
_Header : redirected to parent ZC_MiniMM_StockDocument

Without this redirect, OData navigation from the header to its items would traverse back to the interface entities (ZI_), not the projection entities (ZC_). The service binding would still activate, but navigation in Fiori would either fail silently or return data without the metadata annotations your UI depends on.

Value Help Annotations

The final piece of the CDS layer is the value help definitions, which live in the metadata extension of the item projection view.

 
 
abap
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Material',
  entity.element: 'MaterialID' }]MaterialID;
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Storage',
  entity.element: 'StorageID' }]SourceStorage;
@Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{
  entity.name: 'ZC_MiniMM_Storage',
  entity.element: 'StorageID' }]TargetStorage;

The annotation always points to the projection view (ZC_), not the interface view. This is because the value help dropdown in Fiori is itself an OData call, and OData services are generated from projection views. Pointing at ZI_ would fail at activation or produce no results.

Both SourceStorage and TargetStorage reference the same ZC_MiniMM_Storage projection — same entity, same element, different field. The framework handles this correctly.

Running This on BTP Trial

Everything described in this post runs on a free SAP BTP Trial account with the ABAP environment service instance. No S/4HANA system required. No license. The ABAP Development Tools (ADT) in Eclipse is the only tool you need locally.

If you've been waiting for the right time to start learning ABAP Cloud and RAP, a BTP trial account is genuinely the right place to start. The constraints of the cloud environment (no classic ABAP, no access to SAP standard tables directly) force you to learn the correct patterns from the beginning.

ArchitectureMaterial View InterfaceMaterial Create InterfaceStock Movement Interface

entry.title: How to rename an OData V4 service?
entry.content:
Introduction

I was approached by a colleague from SAP Fiori Elements development who noticed that it is possible to change the  service name of an OData V4 service in an OData V4 service binding.

As a result the OData service URL will change as well, which would mean that an existing deployed app will not work anymore.

Result in short

Original service URL

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0001/

Service URL if the service is being renamed

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/0001/

Service URL's if an additional service definition is being created and a new service version based on that new service definition is being created

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0002/
/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0001/

Service URL if a new service binding is being created for the new service definition 

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/0001/

This has the same effect from a service URL perspective than renaming the service in an existing service definition.

Trying it out 

Using the "from Scratch Wizard" I generated an OData V4 UI service using the structure /DMO/TRAVEL as a template and created a service binding ZUI_TRAVEL_O4.

Using the OData V4 catalog service we can find out more details about this service.

(I just use relative URL's to the hostname of the SAP BTP ABAP Environment system being used. But the same can be tried out in an on premise system as well.)

/sap/opu/odata4/iwfnd/config/default/iwfnd/catalog/0002/ServiceGroups?$expand=DefaultSystem($expand=Services)&$top=500&$filter=GroupId%20eq%20%27ZUI_TRAVEL_O4%27

The response when having published the service looks like follows:

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ServiceGroups(DefaultSystem(Services()))",
  "@odata.metadataEtag": "W/\"20260211141301\"",
  "value": [
    {
      "GroupId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
      "Description": "R3TR G4BA ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
      "DefaultSystem": {
        "SystemAlias": "LOCAL",
        "Description": "Current Client",
        "Services": [
          {
            "RepositoryId": "DEFAULT",
            "ServiceId": "/IWBEP/COMMON",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": null,
            "IsReleasedC2": false,

          },
          {

            "ServiceVersion": "0001",

          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}

 

Option 1 - Renaming the service

When you try to rename the service in the service binding you first will find out that this doesn't work when the service has already been published. 

When the service is unpublished you can rename it, e.g. to e.g. ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF.

After you have performed this change you have to press the publish locally button again.

This then changes the Service Alias and hence the OData ServiceURL. 

The Service URL if the service is being renamed is now:

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/0001/

When checking the entry in the service catalog we will find that the service catalog entry is still there but contains a different service URL.

{
  "@odata.context": "$metadata#ServiceGroups(DefaultSystem(Services()))",
  "@odata.metadataEtag": "W/\"20260211141301\"",
  "value": [
    {
      "GroupId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
      "Description": "R3TR G4BA ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
      "DefaultSystem": {
        "SystemAlias": "LOCAL",
        "Description": "Current Client",
        "Services": [
          {
            "RepositoryId": "DEFAULT",
            "ServiceId": "/IWBEP/COMMON",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": null,
            "IsReleasedC2": false,
            "ServiceAlias": "",
            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0000",
            "Description": "V4 Common Service",
            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/default/iwbep/common/0001/?sap-client=100"
          },
          {
            "RepositoryId": "SRVD",
            "ServiceId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": "UI",
            "IsReleasedC2": false,
            "ServiceAlias": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF",
            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0001",
            "Description": "Service Definition for ZC_TRAVEL",
            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/0001/?sap-client=100"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}

 

Option 2 - Creating another service version

Instead of renaming the service it is possible to create a second service version in the same service binding. For this you have however to create a new service definition. So we simply copy the existing service definition ZUI_TRAVEL_O4  and create an additional service definition. to ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF.

Service Definition ZUI_TRAVEL_O4

@EndUserText: {
  label: 'Service Definition for ZC_TRAVEL'
}
@ObjectModel: {
  leadingEntity: {
    name: 'ZC_TRAVEL'
  }
}
define service ZUI_TRAVEL_O4 provider contracts odata_v4_ui {
  expose ZC_TRAVEL as Travel;
}

Service Definition ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF

Here we expose an additional CDS view so that both services can be better distinguised.

@EndUserText: {
  label: 'Service Definition for ZC_TRAVEL'
}
@ObjectModel: {
  leadingEntity: {
    name: 'ZC_TRAVEL'
  }
}
define service ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF
  provider contracts odata_v4_ui {
  expose ZC_TRAVEL  as Travel;
  expose I_Currency as MyCurrency;
}

When checking the service binding we see two version of our service, 1.0.0 and 2.0.0.

The service version 2.0.0 exposes two entities as expected.

 

And the service URL's differ by the service version 0001 and 0002

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0001/
/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0002/

When checking the OData V4 service catalog we find an additional entry in our service group ZUI_TRAVEL_O4.

{

  "@odata.metadataEtag": "W/\"20260211141301\"",
  "value": [
    {

      "Description": "R3TR G4BA ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
      "DefaultSystem": {
        "SystemAlias": "LOCAL",
        "Description": "Current Client",
        "Services": [
          {
            "RepositoryId": "DEFAULT",
            "ServiceId": "/IWBEP/COMMON",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": null,
            "IsReleasedC2": false,
            "ServiceAlias": "",
            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0000",
            "Description": "V4 Common Service",
            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/default/iwbep/common/0001/?sap-client=100"
          },
          {
            "RepositoryId": "SRVD",
            "ServiceId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": "UI",
            "IsReleasedC2": false,
            "ServiceAlias": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0001",
            "Description": "Service Definition for ZC_TRAVEL",
            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0001/?sap-client=100"
          },
          {
            "RepositoryId": "SRVD",
            "ServiceId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": "UI",
            "IsReleasedC2": false,
            "ServiceAlias": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4",
            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0002",
            "Description": "Service Definition for ZC_TRAVEL",
            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4/0002/?sap-client=100"
          }
        ]

  ]
}

 

Option 3 - Creating another service 

Another option would be to create a new service binding ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF from our new service definition ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF. 

Here the service URL is named 

/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/0001/

So this has from a OData Service URL perspective the same effect as when you rename the existing service.

It has however the big advantage of not being that disruptive and you can decide either to publish a second app using the new URL or to perform the changes to the app only after having transported the newly created service to production.

In order to find your service in the service catalog you have to change the filter as follows:

/sap/opu/odata4/iwfnd/config/default/iwfnd/catalog/0002/ServiceGroups?$expand=DefaultSystem($expand=Services)&$top=500&$filter=GroupId%20eq%20%27ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF%27

and would get the following result.

  "@odata.metadataEtag": "W/\"20260211141301\"",
  "value": [
    {
      "GroupId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF",
      "Description": "R3TR G4BA ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF",
      "DefaultSystem": {
        "SystemAlias": "LOCAL",
        "Description": "Current Client",
        "Services": [
          {
            "RepositoryId": "DEFAULT",
            "ServiceId": "/IWBEP/COMMON",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",
            "ServiceType": null,
            "IsReleasedC2": false,
            "ServiceAlias": "",
            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0000",
            "Description": "V4 Common Service",
            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/default/iwbep/common/0001/?sap-client=100"
          },
          {
            "RepositoryId": "SRVD",
            "ServiceId": "ZUI_TRAVEL_O4_AF",
            "ServiceVersion": "0001",

            "IsReleasedC2": false,

            "ServiceAliasVersion": "0001",

            "ServiceUrl": "/sap/opu/odata4/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/srvd/sap/zui_travel_o4_af/0001/?sap-client=100"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
